question: [File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user target an accountId that is a known high-activity airdrop sender (publicly visible on-chain), causing repository.findAll() to return the maximum result set size on every call and consuming disproportionate network bandwidth and serialization CPU on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the Sort.Direction parameter by switching between ASC and DESC on consecutive requests for the same accountId, causing the DB query optimizer to generate different execution plans for each direction and preventing stable plan caching, increasing planning overhead per query?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user craft a TokenAirdropRequest with a serialNumbers Bound that uses an EQ operator on a non-existent serial number, causing the DB to perform a full index scan to confirm the absence of the value rather than using a point lookup, wasting DB CPU on every such request?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the AirdropRequestType.PENDING path where SENDER_ACCOUNT_ID becomes the primaryField (sort field) rather than the baseField, causing the query to sort by a field that is not the primary filter condition and requiring an additional sort operation on the result set, increasing CPU usage?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user issue getAirdrops() requests with a limit value at the maximum boundary combined with a wide tokenIds range, causing the DB to scan a large index range and then apply a LIMIT clause late in execution (after scanning many rows), wasting I/O on rows that are ultimately discarded?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the entityService.lookup() call by providing accountId values that are valid but resolve to different internal entity IDs on each request (e.g., using different alias formats for the same account), causing repeated cache misses in the entity lookup layer and increasing DB load?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user craft requests that combine AirdropRequestType.OUTSTANDING with a wide entityIds Bound (receiver ID range from 0 to max), causing the query to use RECEIVER_ACCOUNT_ID as the primary sort field with a very broad range, resulting in a near-full index scan of the token_airdrop table?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the Collection<TokenAirdrop> return type by targeting an account with thousands of pending airdrops, causing the JVM to allocate a large number of TokenAirdrop objects in heap memory simultaneously, triggering frequent garbage collection cycles that increase CPU overhead on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user send requests with a tokenIds Bound using a LTE operator with Long.MAX_VALUE as the upper bound, causing the query to treat all token IDs as valid and perform a full scan of the token_airdrop index for the given account, returning the maximum possible result set?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the getBounds() method's conditional logic by providing only serialNumbers as a non-empty Bound (leaving entityIds and tokenIds empty), causing getBounds() to return List.of(serialNumbers) and forcing the repository to filter only by serial number without account or token constraints, resulting in an extremely broad and expensive DB scan?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user issue sustained getAirdrops() requests targeting different high-volume accounts in a round-robin pattern, causing the DB buffer pool to continuously evict warm pages and load cold pages, degrading the overall cache efficiency of the mirror node DB and increasing I/O for all concurrent users?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user craft a TokenAirdropRequest with entityIds Bound using a GTE operator with value 0 and no upper bound, causing the repository to generate a query with an open-ended range condition on the primary sort field that scans all receiver/sender IDs greater than 0 for the given account, consuming excessive DB resources?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the lack of connection-level throttling on getAirdrops() by opening multiple HTTP connections and issuing concurrent requests, each targeting a different high-cardinality accountId, causing the DB connection pool to be saturated with expensive long-running queries and starving other operations?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the AirdropRequestType enum switching between OUTSTANDING (baseField=SENDER_ACCOUNT_ID) and PENDING (baseField=RECEIVER_ACCOUNT_ID) to cause the DB to alternate between two different index access paths, preventing the DB from building a stable query execution plan cache and increasing per-query planning overhead?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user craft requests where the tokenIds Bound and serialNumbers Bound are both non-empty but entityIds is empty, causing getBounds() to return List.of(tokenIds, serialNumbers) and forcing the repository to execute a two-level range query that is less selective than an account-scoped query, scanning a larger portion of the table?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that getAirdrops() calls entityService.lookup() synchronously before repository.findAll(), and by providing an accountId that causes a slow entity resolution (e.g., a complex alias lookup), hold a DB connection or thread for an extended period, reducing available thread pool capacity for other requests?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user send getAirdrops() requests with a limit at the maximum value and Sort.Direction.DESC combined with a non-empty serialNumbers Bound, causing the DB to scan the token_airdrop table in reverse serial number order and apply a late LIMIT, reading far more rows than are returned and wasting I/O resources?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the three-bound getBounds() return path by providing entityIds, tokenIds, and serialNumbers all as non-empty Bounds with overlapping ranges, causing the repository to generate a query with redundant or conflicting range conditions that the DB optimizer cannot simplify, resulting in an inefficient execution plan?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user issue getAirdrops() requests targeting an accountId that is a known airdrop aggregator (e.g., a token distribution contract visible on-chain), causing repository.findAll() to consistently return near-maximum result sets and consuming disproportionate serialization and network bandwidth on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user craft a TokenAirdropRequest with a tokenIds Bound using both GTE and LTE operators spanning the entire valid token ID space, combined with AirdropRequestType.PENDING, causing the query to perform a full range scan on the SENDER_ACCOUNT_ID index with no effective token ID filtering, consuming maximum DB I/O?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TokenAirdropService.java -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getAirdrops()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the sequential execution of entityService.lookup() followed by repository.findAll() in getAirdrops() by crafting requests that cause both operations to be slow simultaneously (slow entity resolution + large airdrop result set), maximizing thread hold time and reducing server throughput?
